speaker: interviewer
text: --with Mrs. Alicia Appleman. You were telling me how even if you wanted to leave, you had no where to go. And I also would like to know what happened to your father's business during the Russian occupation and then during the German occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians-- my father was a partner in a factory, which manufactured fabrics and that was in Bielsko. So Poland was occupied. Russia-- I mean, Germany occupied Bielsko in 1939. So he lost his business. He had some fabrics, which he had to give up to the Russians. But it was amazing. Of all things, they let my father manage a-- a china store of fine china, which was imported from Czechoslovakia. I-- I was very amazed. He was a very fine manager, so they let him do that. And whenever I came back from school in the afternoon, I-- I came over to my father. My father taught me how to identify fine china. So he worked in that store, and this is how he made his livelihood. But we had to give up most of the fabrics that we had at home to the Russians. This is how he supported us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then during the Germans'?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the German occupation, my father died within the first few weeks of the occupation. The only way we could live is if we sold things from the house. But what happened, the Germans took over homes when they came into my hometown. And one of the homes they took over was ours. What they really came to take is some artwork-- pictures that we had, very fine pictures in our home. My grandfather's brother was an art collector. He lived in Holland. He was a ship builder and an art collector. He supplied all these pictures to our family. But saw it was such a lovely home. So instead of taking the pictures out, they threw us out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about four weeks after the Germans entered my hometown. It was a terrible day. But I wanted to take our album, but the German pulled it out of my hands. The pictures spilled, and he stepped on them. And I wanted to say something. My brother stopped me because he would have shot me right there and then. And you know, for many, many years I could not put pictures in albums. I was-- I-- I has such a memory, such a painful memory. And then we moved over to a different house, what latter on turned out to be to the ghetto. And so we lost our home at a very early time. And we lived in-- in this. We had two rooms and then we just lived in one room. Ours was not a closed-in ghetto. Ours was an open ghetto. They weren't-- they weren't barbed wires or anything. It ended at the main street. To-- you could go to the main street and this was it. You could not cross the main street. They, of course, didn't give us any food. We didn't have any food rations at all. The only way you could get any food is to take some of your clothing-- to endanger your life-- take off the Star of David, go to the market, and trade it there with the farmers. And of course, we children realized if our parents were to go out, they would be caught and killed. So we organized a [? chain ?] time, and we children went out to the market. And we traded for-- for food. And many of these children were caught, and they were killed. And you know, I want to tell you something that I have never seen such brave children in this world as the children in our ghetto. I called them the little stars from the kingdom of darkness. They were, indeed, little stars. They left us a beautiful light, a light of bravery. Children, you know, some of them were as young as six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall an incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I know of one little boy who was caught, and he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I did not see it with my own eyes, but what I did see with my own eyes, my young cousin-- my-- my father's cousin who was about to give birth-- was very close to giving birth-- she went to the market with her little five-year-old girl. And she was caught by a German, and she was killed right there. Actually, he slit her stomach open, and he bayoneted the little girl. I-- that, I saw with my own eyes, and I testified against that murderer in 1961. I was living in Florida. I saw it with my own eyes. And children just disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't tell my-- my mother at all. I had terrible nightmares, terrible nightmares. I still have terrible nightmares. But I continued going to the market, because our life depended. There was a man who was manufacturing soap. So I was taking the soap from him, selling the soap for the same price I paid him. But I asked the farmer to give me a piece of bread. You see, at that time, my brother, Bunio was taken to a camp, and we had to send him some food. And the only time I could get any bread is whenever I sold this soap, and I got a piece of bread in addition to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say your brother, Bunio, was taken to a camp. When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes [? there ?] was. They-- they came into the ghetto, and they gathered up about 60 young boys-- 15, 16 year olds. And they sent them to a camp called [? Borkiev ?] [? Yelki. ?] Now, this was supposed to be a camp-- a work camp, and it was a work camp. But one of the boys escaped. And then they took all of the boys, and they asked them to make the circle. And they counted to 10-- like 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10. And they shot every 10th boy. And my brother, Bunio, was the 10th boy. And I found out, and I had to tell my mother. It was truly devastating. It was a terrible thing. I remember crying for my brother nights and nights crying for him. But it was also a point where I swore to myself that if I lived, I shall protect my mother and my little brother with my life. Because we had already at that time lost Zachary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talking about Zachary you said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, after there was one action in our hometown where they fell upon the homes. They took people to the railway station and put them on the train. I was taken in this action. I was playing chess with two boys-- a boy 10 and 11-- I was at the doctor's home. Their father--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was November, I believe it was. And that was the first action, the first time they took people away. The first-- the only time-- they never took people to the railway station again. They fell upon this house, and I remember the door flew open. We were playing chess. The door flew open and two SS men walked in and they gave the command everybody out. Now the father of these boys-- they were refugees from Germany, he was a physician, a family friend-- realized what was happening so he went over to one of them and begged them. He said this little girl is not my daughter please let her go. But he wouldn't hear of it, and they pushed us out. And then they pushed-- I-- I saw other people being pushed out of the homes. And then beatings and killings started. Those who survived were taken to the railway station, and there was a freight train waiting for us there. And there were soldiers, German soldiers. We call them now Nazis. I had never heard the word 'Nazi.' To me, they were soldiers who came to kill us. There was the SS. There was the Gestapo there, the police, with dogs. And then they let the dogs loose on us. And we had nowhere to go but inside these freight cars, and they locked them from the outside. And the train took off. After a while, I heard some commotion inside the car. You see, European freight trains have windows. Later on when the Jews were taken, from what I have learned in history to concentration camps, they had barbed wire. But not in our cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the men-- adults promptly realized that if they could spread the bars, they could throw some children out-- maybe save some lives. So the-- the father of these boys turned to said, Alicia, you're next. And this is what they were doing-- throwing children out through this window. He says, Alicia, you are next. And I said what do you mean I am next? What about your own sons? And then he looked at me, and I'll never forget the look in his face. He said, I swore to save humanity, but humanity's gone insane. And you are next.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I didn't even have a chance to say I want to go, I don't want to go. He just picked me up, handed me into a man's arms and called after me roll, roll. Follow the tracks home. And then another man picked me up, and I was pushed through that window. I was flying there for-- for a second or two, and then I suddenly hit something very heavy. And then I don't remember exactly how I came home. I remember-- I remember the river. And he told me to follow the tracks home, so I remember that I was walking on the tracks. And I was-- and the train turned around that was trying to catch me. And I was screaming and screaming. You see, I did get home. I was unconscious. I had hit my head. I had a concussion. I was unconscious for a whole week, and then I finally woke up. And now that my brother, Zachary, told me that another little girl also returned, but she died of internal hemorrhaging. I-- at the time, we had absolutely no idea where the train was going. There wasn't any other train from my hometown ever taken again. But I was the only survivor of what later on became known to us as actions-- in German, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't recall how you got home? Or how long it took?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- I remember the river. I remember talking to somebody, but I don't remember. I had the concussion, so I must have been-- the train wasn't very far from our home. That is-- that is true too. But I don't quite remember. I remember following the tracks, but I woke up after a week. That's all I remembered, but I do know how they threw me out through the window. And of course, later on, my brother told me that most of the children who were thrown out of the window where shot in flight. Because the Germans saw them being thrown out through the windows. They must have been doing it in other cars as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this incident happen before your brother, Bunio, was taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was the first action. And that happened when my brother, Bunio, was taken away in the beginning of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first, your brother Moshe then your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Bunio.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then Bunio. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother take all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was devastating for my mother. She tried to be brave. But-- and I had to give her the news also about Zachary-- what happened to Zachary. I had to give her all this news. It was terrible, absolutely devastating. But she continued, you know, showing face for us, but at night, I could hear her crying. She was a very-- now that I am a mother myself, I realize her pain. Devastating. She was extremely brave to be able to-- to care for us all together. Not only did she care for us, but my mother picked up children in the ghetto whose parents were taken into an action. And if the children survived, she brought them to our home. She cared for other children as well. Was a very kind lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take to the ghetto with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took to the ghetto very few things. Some of the things that my-- my-- my mother took. My-- my father, at the time, was-- was not-- yeah, my father was still alive. My brothers too. But I took some sketches that I made and-- with me and some books. We knew already that-- there were-- there were rumors that there's going to be a ghetto. But we were thrown out suddenly out of our home. They sealed our home, but I think my mother and my brothers went back to take some of the things out. But I don't know of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you moved to the ghetto, you still were a complete family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when we moved to the ghetto we were. Because they threw us out of our home. It wasn't exactly that we moved to the ghetto at that time. It just happened to be that this part turned into the ghetto. We had to move out of our home. They threw us out of our home, so we moved to a new place. But at that time, it wasn't a ghetto yet. It turned into a ghetto later. And this house fell under this area of the ghetto. But we were evicted from our home because the Germans wanted our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long after did the incident with your father-- your father was taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think my father-- to be very correct, I think my father wasn't with us then. Right, my father wasn't with us then because my father was taken away about three weeks after the Germans came in. So it was-- my father wasn't-- my brother Zachary was there in the room when the Germans came, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before that your brother Zachary-- something also happen to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the young--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when-- when was this, related to the time you moved into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the beginning of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there other incidents that come before that that you'd like to tell me first?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, we had the action, the-- the 600 men were killed in our hometown. The train-- the train left my hometown in 1941. And then we went into a-- into a miserable winter of 1942. And the-- the young people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, you were living in the ghetto still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that is when they ghetto was being formed, in 1942. But we were living there because we were evicted from our home. So we were living there even before the ghetto was formerly declared. We were living already in that area. The young people did not take all of the German occupation lightly. They were organizing. They knew that eventually they will have to organize a resistance. And so my brother Zachary and his friend started organizing. They-- I don't think they could get hold of-- of any ammunitions. But what they could do, however, is have informations whenever action would start and whenever people went into hiding to protect the people when they were hidden. And so my brother and his friends, whenever the Jewish people-- because we knew that trouble was starting. And people who had friends-- farmers or-- or anybody who lived in villages-- they gave all they had. And the people promised to hide them. But after a while, they took everything they had. And they told them they were taking them to a safer place. But instead, they took them all to the police. The police killed them. Or they killed them themselves. And my brother and his friends were aware of this. And then they went to the farmers, and they burned the farmers' barn or beat the farmers up as a retaliation so that people who were hiding Jews should get the message that you can't just, you know, betray them. And one day, he heard of a family that was going to be betrayed who had four children. So my brother, Zachary, went to bring them back into the ghetto, because it was better for them to be in the ghetto than to be killed by these supposingly protectors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he tell you he was going to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I found out from his friends what-- how he died later on, but I did not see it myself. But I know that my brother was caught, and he was hung. All-- he was betrayed by a Polish boy who was an intermediary who-- who was supposed to be helping out. Because my brother was missing from home for three days. Sometimes he was missing for half a day or a day, but he was never away for three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I went looking from everywhere, and I couldn't find him. And then towards the end, in the afternoon, I came across two boys and two girls who were standing on the street. And-- and I heard one of the girls say here comes his little sister, here comes the little sister. And I found out-- I asked them what about to the little sister? And one of the boys stepped forward to say, Alicia, I will tell you. I'll take you to your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we passed the bridge, and we came to the prison yard. And there was my brother hanging. And I sat near him. And the Ukrainian policeman came out. He pulled out his gun and put it to my head and said [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I will shoot you. And I say, [NON-ENGLISH]. Shoot. And the following day, he took out the gun and put it to my head, and I said go ahead and shoot. I don't know why he didn't shoot me. But in the evening we-- when the prison-- when the policeman went into the prison, my brother's friends came. They cut my brother down. We brought him to the cemetery. I didn't even let my mother know because my brother was mutilated. His hands were broken, and we buried him. And I swore on his grave that if I lived, I shall tell his story and the story of many brave boys and girls who fought very valiantly the way they knew how to fight. And I have literally dedicated my life to telling their story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before-- before being hanged, your brother was also tortured?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But he never revealed anything, because no one was ever arrested. Because they knew that my brother belonged to a resistance group, but he never revealed anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who hanged him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, sweetheart. It could have been the Germans and Ukrainians together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there he was hanging in the prison yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to tell my mother. It was devastating. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is-- and then, of course, my brother, Bunio-- my brother Zachary was the first to go and then Bunio. And then just my mother and my little brother were left. And I swore on my brother's grave that-- and I promised myself that I will protect my mother and my little brother with my life. If anybody has to die in the family, I will be the one to die. But I am not going to-- to-- to watch my mother or bring her news of-- of another tragedy. There were actions during that period of time. And then in 1942, the winter of 1942, it was the beginning of December. I remember we lived in-- in a little house. There were about 16 people, and the only clothing available to all of us was my mother's shawl, her jacket, and her shoes. And I put them on, and I went to pump some water for our household. And that just came in and put the pails down in the hall. I was about to enter our room. Then suddenly the door-- there was a knock on the door, and the door flew open. And a Ukrainian policeman entered. And he had the yellow pad in his hand. He called my mother's name, Frieda Jurman. You see, I was all bundled up and a tall girl. And I said, yes. And I bolted, and I started running.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he called after me, hey, where are you going to? You shouldn't be running. But you see, I had to run. I had to get this policeman away from the house, because my mother was in the room there. And I ran all the way, and then finally when he locked me [INAUDIBLE ]-- because he thought he-- he had Frieda Jurman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he locked me in the prison cell. I said, thank god. The prison cell was filled with mothers. And later on, I found out they had taken fathers too. And so in the early morning around 5 o'clock, they took us out of the prison cells into a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a policeman sitting behind a desk. He had a yellow pad. And people passed in front of him. They have to give their names, and he registered them. My turn came, and I gave my name Alicia Jurman. And the policeman lifted up his head and said, Alicia, what are you doing here? This is not for children. They're going to take you to a terrible place. When the Germans go-- come in, go down on your knees, beg for your life. Maybe they will let you go because you are child. But as he was talking, I was getting angrier by the moment. Do you know who he was? He was my friend, Olga's father. He was the man who when I went to pick Olga up to go to school with her, he hugged me and kissed me. He-- he said he loved me like a daughter, and I loved them like a father.

speaker: interviewee
text: But here he was sitting-- this traitor-- not just registering us but you see, he was a Ukrainian policeman who came with the Germans into the ghetto to kill us, to take us to the graves. And my heart just froze, kind of a darkness settled into me. And I stood against the wall. And then they came-- you have seen them on television-- in those green uniforms. To me, they look like angels of death. And I said to myself, never. I will never go down on my knees. I will never beg for my life. I would rather die as a proud Jewish girl. And I stood there, and Olga's father realized that I'm not going to beg for my life. He jumped up and just smacked me over the face. And then they took us out. It was still dark outside. They had sleighs waiting for us. They put us on the sleighs. And the Ukrainian policemen sat around us with their guns on our backs. And when we left the prison yard I knew if we were going to go to the right, they will take us to the [? feather ?] in the forest and kill us where my father died. But they took us to the left, and we passed the bridge. We came to the center of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked up where the ghetto was-- it was on a slight hill-- and I started to cry. Because suddenly I realized the anguish my mother must be feeling in her heart when she saw the pails of water but where was her daughter. And do you know what I wished that morning? I just wished that the sun wouldn't come out, and there would be total darkness in this world as there was darkness in my young heart. But the sun came out. It was a beautiful December day. When we passed-- you know, we were passing the villages. I remember the children were going to school, and they were looking at us so strangely. What are these people surrounded by police? What kind of criminals are being taken away? And I remember some of them waved to us, and some of them just made faces. Who were the people watching you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmers, especially the children. I-- I was-- I felt such anguish in my heart, and I felt such injustice. Because I was supposed to be going to school too. And I wasn't a criminal. I didn't do anything wrong. My-- my only crime was that I was a Jewish girl, which I did not consider a crime. And then I thought what an unjust world that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, these-- these bullies came. These Germans came with guns and machine guns and declared war on us Jewish children. I mean, what kind of fight is that? What kind of justice is that? You know, if they had sent us their Hitlerjugend, we would have whooped the pants off them. But they can't themselves. What could children do? And I felt very unjust at that point. So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took us. We traveled all morning, and then they brought us to the city of [? Troy ?] [? Groeven-- ?] put up in the same prison where my brother, Moshe was imprisoned. And they brought us into a hall, asked us to make a circle. And then two SS men went to the back to collect the valuables. You know, I didn't see any valuables. I just-- they took everything from us by that time, but some parents still had their wedding rings. So they threw them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came in front of me-- they both came in front of me-- and I had on a pair of earrings my mother gave me when I was maybe five years old. I took one earring off, and I threw it in the bag. I tried to take the other one off, and I couldn't-- just didn't-- couldn't loosen it. So I asked-- because, you know, I spoke perfect German-- I asked him may have some time please?

speaker: interviewee
text: But one of them just stepped forward and tore the earring off. So I told them, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You are a devil. And then he hit me and threw me on the floor and stepped on me and just stepped over and over again until I lost consciousness. The next thing I remember waking up in the prison cell. I was bleeding from my mouth. And all my body was hurting me, because he just-- he cracked all my ribs. And there was in this cell, and then I remember the door opened. And they gave us water to drink, and they urged us to drink this water. And then I found out later on it was contaminated with typhoid germs.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what I remember-- you know, there was a window in my prison cell. And there was a bird pecking in the snow. And I said to the bird, you know what, bird? They're going to kill me here, so I'm going to give you my soul. At least my soul will be free. And then I changed my mind, and I said to the bird, no, I am going to try to live one minute after these murderers pay for their crimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was such a need in me to see justice done. And then very soon-- and I heard-- I was in that prison cell-- and then I heard terrible cries. They were taking people out from the cell and beating them and then bringing them-- some returned, some didn't. But I was already quite ill at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with in the cell?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with the-- Mrs. Eckerling, Mrs. Eckerling-- Mrs.-- a lady. Actually she was-- her husband and my father were in business together. And she came when the Germans [? attacked, ?] but she came to my hometown, to Buchach. She died there. And then soon, one day the door opened and more SS men came in. And then one came close to me, and then all I remember was the boots with those iron spike coming towards me. And I remember a tremendous amount of pain. And then I just blacked out. I just sank. Next thing, I woke up about two weeks later.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going into the next tape now.
